result: I keep returning to the idea that the universe is a library written in wind. Every gust through the pines is a footnote, every thunderclap a forgotten annotation. We walk between the shelves, trailing fingers across spines we can’t quite read, mouths full of borrowed syllables. Children invent colors that crayons haven’t caught up with yet; I eavesdrop on their sidewalk treaties—trading a marble galaxy for a single sycopter wing—because barter is the only honest economy left. My grandmother folds dumplings like origami moons, sealing minced memories inside doughy satellites, and I wonder if taste buds are just another script. Meanwhile, smartphones jitter with panic, begging for nightly juice like small, anxious pets. We scroll so fast that time liquefies; yesterday’s earthquake is already demoted to a thumbnail. I’d like to slow the frames, press my palm to the screen until pixels pulse back into heartbeat, until the woman under rubble becomes someone’s sister again, not just content. Outside, the catalpa drops cigar-shaped pods onto the roof, percussion for a season that refuses naming. I collect them in a jar, pretending they’re messages I’ll decrypt when the moon learns Morse. Really, they’re blanks, and that’s the comfort: possibilities unspent, like subway tokens in a city that’s switched to QR codes. Somewhere, a whale sings at the wrong frequency, unanswered. I answer by humming into a coffee mug, letting ceramic echo back what loneliness sounds like when amplified by porcelain. Tomorrow I’ll misplace this moment, but tonight it’s overdue, accruing interest in the bank of almost.

Children invent colors that crayons haven’t caught up with yet; I eavesdrop on their sidewalk treaties—trading a marble galaxy for a single sycopter wing—because barter is the only honest economy left. My grandmother folds dumplings like origami moons, sealing minced memories inside doughy satellites, and I wonder if taste buds are just another script. Meanwhile, smartphones jitter with panic, begging for nightly juice like small, anxious pets. We scroll so fast that time liquefies; yesterday’s earthquake is already demoted to a thumbnail. I’d like to slow the frames, press my palm to the screen until pixels pulse back into heartbeat, until the woman under rubble becomes someone’s sister again, not just content. Outside, the catalpa drops cigar-shaped pods onto the roof, percussion for a season that refuses naming. I collect them in a jar, pretending they’re messages I’ll decrypt when the moon learns Morse. Really, they’re blanks, and that’s the comfort: possibilities unspent, like subway tokens in a city that’s switched to QR codes. Somewhere, a whale sings at the wrong frequency, unanswered. I answer by humming into a coffee mug, letting ceramic echo back what loneliness sounds like when amplified by porcelain. Tomorrow I’ll misplace this moment, but tonight it’s overdue, accruing interest in the bank of almost.
